header.title: sokech.com
header.subtitle: Personal portfolio and resume site deployed on AWS S3 + CloudFront with automated CI/CD and a self-healing DNS automation layer

summary.plain_english: A static personal portfolio site built with Vite and vanilla JavaScript, deployed globally via AWS CloudFront with private S3 origins and OAC. All infrastructure is defined as code with AWS CDK, DNS is automated through a Lambda-backed CloudFormation custom resource that provisions Cloudflare CNAMEs on every deploy, and a GitHub Actions pipeline handles build, PDF generation, and deployment with OIDC authentication — no AWS credentials stored anywhere.

motivation.quote: I wanted a portfolio that demonstrates the same cloud engineering practices I apply to production projects — IaC, OIDC CI/CD, CDN delivery, and automated DNS — rather than just hosting a static page on a third-party platform.

architecture.diagram_alt: Architecture diagram showing visitor traffic flowing through Cloudflare DNS CNAME Flattening to CloudFront, which serves content from a private S3 bucket via OAC. GitHub Actions uses OIDC to assume an IAM role for deployments. A Lambda-backed CDK custom resource provisions Cloudflare DNS CNAMEs automatically on every stack deploy.
architecture.flow_summary: Visitor → Cloudflare DNS (CNAME Flattening) → CloudFront (HTTPS, HTTP/2+3, OAC) → S3 private bucket | GitHub Actions → OIDC → IAM Role → S3 sync + CloudFront invalidation | CDK custom resource → Lambda → Cloudflare API → DNS CNAMEs

items: The site is served globally through CloudFront from a private S3 origin — content lives in a private S3 bucket accessed via OAC (no public ACLs); GitHub Actions syncs the bucket and invalidates the CloudFront cache on every push to main with no manual steps

DNS provisioning is fully automated on every CDK deploy — since DNS is managed by Cloudflare rather than Route 53, a Lambda-backed CloudFormation custom resource calls the Cloudflare API to upsert CNAMEs for sokech.com, www, and assets.sokech.com; DNS stays self-healing without any manual intervention

Project media is served from a dedicated CDN under assets.sokech.com — a separate CloudFront distribution and S3 bucket handles screenshots, diagrams, and demo videos, with CORS headers configured so assets can be embedded across subdomains like cloudclips.sokech.com

The downloadable resume is generated automatically at CI time — before the Vite build step, a Puppeteer script reads site.json and all project JSON blueprints and renders a formatted A4 PDF via headless Chromium; the PDF is a build artifact so it can never drift out of sync with the site content

Cloudflare DNS with CloudFront at the root domain: Cloudflare's CNAME Flattening resolves the CNAME at the DNS level for the apex domain, which is normally incompatible with standard CNAME records. Using DNS-only mode (grey cloud, not orange) in Cloudflare was critical — proxying through Cloudflare would conflict with CloudFront's own TLS termination.

ACM certificate automation with Cloudflare DNS: CDK's native Certificate construct only supports Route 53 for DNS validation. I wrote a Lambda-backed CloudFormation Custom Resource that requests the ACM certificate, reads the DNS validation CNAME from the ACM API, and provisions it in Cloudflare — polling until issuance before signalling CloudFormation success.

Resume PDF always in sync: keeping the downloadable resume manually updated alongside JSON content changes is error-prone. Solved by generating the PDF from site.json at CI time using Puppeteer, so the PDF is a build artifact rather than a committed file that can drift out of date.

What I'd do differently: add a CloudFront Function to handle URL rewrites (trailing slashes, .html extensions) at the edge rather than relying on S3 error response redirects, which adds latency and can cause issues with deep-linked routes in the future.
